title: The WEIRDest People in the World: How the West Became Psychologically Peculiar and Particularly Prosperous
excerpt: A sweeping account of how the Catholic Church's medieval marriage policies dismantled kin-based institutions in Western Europe, creating the individualistic, analytical, and impersonally prosocial psychology that made the modern world possible.
keyIdeas: WEIRD (Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic) people are psychological outliers — more individualistic, analytic, trusting of strangers, and rule-oriented than most humans who have ever lived
The Catholic Church's Marriage and Family Program (MFP) — bans on cousin marriage, polygyny, and adoption — systematically dismantled Europe's kin-based institutions over 1,500 years
Without clan backing, isolated nuclear families developed impersonal prosociality, analytical thinking, and trust in abstract rules and institutions
Cultural evolution, not rational design or racial superiority, explains the psychological peculiarities that drove the Great Divergence
keyTakeaways: Western psychology is unusual in global and historical context — what feels "normal" to WEIRD people is actually quite strange
The Catholic Church accidentally shaped modern individualism through marriage prohibitions that dissolved kin networks
Impersonal markets, voluntary associations, and rule of law emerged from the psychological shift toward non-kin cooperation
Cultural evolution is a powerful lens for explaining history — institutions shape psychology, which in turn shapes institutions
whoShouldRead: Students of psychology, anthropology, and history seeking a unified framework for understanding Western peculiarity
Readers of Sapiens and Guns, Germs, and Steel who want the next big-picture theory of how the West became different
Anyone interested in cultural evolution, religion, family structures, or the deep roots of individualism
Policy thinkers wondering why Western institutions do not always transplant easily to other cultural contexts
whoShouldSkip: Readers wanting a tightly focused historical narrative (Henrich ranges across 1,500 years and multiple disciplines)
Those uncomfortable with evolutionary and psychological explanations for large-scale historical patterns
Specialist medieval historians looking for archival depth rather than broad-brush social science synthesis
